speaker: interviewer
text: Just a second. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is June 9th, 1995. I'm Rhoda Daun-Kenner and we are interviewing in New Jersey today, in Springfield, New Jersey, Norman Salsitz, who is our survivor in the United States of America-- Springfield, New Jersey. And the interview is being conducted in English. Today is June 9th, 1995. I'm Rhoda Daun-Kenner doing an interview today with Norman Salsitz, survivor, in Springfield, New Jersey, in the United States of America. And this interview is being conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good afternoon, Mr. Salsitz. I'm delighted to be able to do this interview for posterity for your children and for your grandchildren. Today, for the Shoah Foundation. And let's start by having you tell me your full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is now Norman Salsitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-A-L-S-I-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say now, can you tell me what your given name was when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was born, I-- my name was Naftali Saleschutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-A-L-E-S-C-H-U-T-Z. I have to think about to not to make a mistake.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a small town in, in [NON-ENGLISH]. But as a child, as an infant, we moved to Kolbuszowa, and I was brought up in Kolbuszowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Kolbuszowa, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-L-B-U-S-Z-O-W-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Kolbuszowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kolbuszowa is in the southern part of Poland. It was Galicia. It was known Galicia. In Polish, we called our section Mourpoliska. It was, means minor Poland. And this was halfway from Lviv and Krakow, which were two large towns in Galicia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born May the 6th, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Isak. And we- he was called in Yiddish Itcha.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in 1882.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a small village about five kilometer from Kolbuszowa, which belonged to our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did your father die?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was shot, was killed, the 28th of April, 1942, in our home in the ghetto, in Kolbuszowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our home, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can-- well, we'll come back to that later because I'd like to know the circumstances of that. Can you tell me about your mother, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, her name was Esther. Her maiden name was Berl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-R-L. She was born in Kolbuszowa. And she-- they married-- she-- they married in Kolbuszowa. And my mother died when she was taken away to the ghetto in Rzeszow, in a larger town. And from Rzeszow, she was taken with all my sisters, I had five sisters, and with all the sisters' children, to Belzec, that camp in Belzec, where they were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you mention, spell for us, please, the first camp that you mentioned, and then Belzec?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was not a camp. She--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had the ghetto in our town, in Kolbuszowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, but you said from there, you went somewhere else.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but what the Germans did, they took all Jews from small towns, from small ghettos and they concentrate them in a large town where it was a railroad station to have it-- to be-- at-- they should be able to take them away when there was an akcja. So this town was Rzeszow, the larger town in our section.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Can you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rzeszow is R-Z-E-S-Z-O-W. And from Rzeszow, when they had their thousands and thousands of Jews, they sent-- made transports to Belzec, where they were gassed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, in Belzec, can you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Belzec is B-E-L-Z-E-C. Belzec.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And you say you have-- you had the sisters and brothers. Could you tell me how many children were in the family, and start with the eldest first?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were nine children, and I was the youngest from the nine. My oldest brother, his name was Abgrum. He came to America before I was even born. And he became Albert. And he was the first one who changed the name from Saleschutz to Salsitz. For this reason, my name is Salsitz. Abgrum came to America. He married, got children. And he died natural death about 15 years ago here in the United States. The second was a sister. She was the oldest sister. Her name was Gela. Actually, her name was Bluma Gela, but she was called Gela. She married in 1921, and her name-- she married a man. His name was Ruben Weinstein. They had one daughter, Shaindle who was two years younger than I. They also were killed in Belzec. The, the transport to Belzec from Rzeszow with all my family was in July the 7th, 1942. And a few days later, I don't know exactly the day when they were gassed, but usually it took two or three days till they were gassed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the next-- the next--

speaker: interviewee
text: After Gela came a brother. His name was Leibush. And Leibush was-- he survived the war with me in the woods. He was with me all the time in the ghettos, in camps. And in the woods, he survived. He had a wife and three children. They were taken to Belzec in the same day and were killed. After the war, he remarried. And he has one-- he had one son. His son, his name is Edwin Salsitz. He's a doctor in New York. He works in Beth Israel Hospital. Leibush died 20 years ago, and a natural death in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And after Leibush?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Leibush was a sister, her name was Malcha. We called her Malcha, but her name was Alta Malka. The reason-- well, I don't think so, this is important why her name was Alta Malka, because she was named after an aunt who died very young. And by Jews, if somebody died young, so you gave an additional name or Alta, which means old or Haya, which means life. So her name was Alta Malka, but we called her Malcha. Malcha was married to [? Shi ?] David Leischer And they had two little girls. A girl, Dubcha, Blimcha and Dubcha. And they were also killed in Belzec in the same day, which Dubcha-- Blimcha was three and a half years and Dubcha was two years when they were killed. After Malcha came Liba. Liba was my third sister and she married a man. His name was Moses Kornfeld And they had two small boys, also a three and a half year old boy and a two year old boy. And the boy's names were-- the oldest was Heneh, And the second was Yosel. They also were taken to Belzec in the same day, where they were killed. Then I had a, a sister. And her name was Matla. Martle, we called her. She was not married. And she also perished the same day in Belzec. After Matla, we had a-- I have a brother. His name was David. His full name is [? Shia ?] David. And David was in Palestine. He left our town in 1933 as a pioneer, as a Chalutz. And he was in Palestine, and this is the reason why he survived in Palestine. He was married in Palestine. He has two children. When I came to the United States, he wanted to be together with the family. I brought them over. And he is in the United States. He lives here. His wife died since then, but he is alive. This is the only siblings that I have left. After David was the youngest sister. Her name was Raisel, or in Polish, Ruchla Her full name, [? Reiselghagow, ?] but we called her [? Ragho ?] or [? Raisel. ?] She was my beloved sister. Even all of them, we were very close home. But because she was close in age to me, and she tried to help me with things that a young boy should know, and she was only three years older. And she also perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the same-- all of them at the same time. As I mentioned, [? Raisel, ?] she could survive. And being in the Holocaust, there were-- unfortunately, the Jews have two things that they-- it was a curse for the Jews. One was the closeness and the attachment to the family. Like, for instance, [? Raisel ?] worked in our ghetto because she spoke good German, she wrote good German. She was very intelligent. She spoke English, Hebrew, Yiddish, Polish, German.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when she worked as a secretary to the head of the Arbeitsamt, a German, to the head of the labor community from-- in our ghetto. Can, can, can you hold that story, because I want to go to finish with pre-war before we move to during the war? If you don't mind, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your father. How did he support this family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a very prominent man in our town. To be prominent in a small town, you had to have two important things. First thing, you had to be a scholar because money was not so important. If somebody was a scholar, and if he knew how to study and he-- he was an important man, which he was. He was very religious. This was also important. And the second thing, he was always well off. He had-- we had a wholesale store, a general store. We sold everything. And this was the biggest store in our town. Because the small villages, every village had a small town there. And the villagers who owned a small store, I'm sorry. And the villagers bought merchandise by my father in this wholesale place and they sold it in the villages.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they came to us and we had a very large county. We had almost 70 villages. And they all bought by us the merchandise. In this way, my father prospered. And he was one of the well-to-do Jews in our town. What size town? How many people lived in a town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our town-- out town had 4,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 2,000 Jews and 2,000 Poles. It was 50/50.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, can you tell me was your father involved in the Jewish community in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was involved in everything, as I am involved here in everything. And everybody who knows me, they say you're exactly like your father. There was not an organization, there was not an institution, there was not-- anything, my father was involved. And he was involved not only in Jewish organizations or in Jewish institutions, also in non-Jewish organizations. He was always a member of city council. And I said-- at one time, he was the deputy mayor, where a Jew couldn't be a mayor. So always was a Pole a mayor and a Jew was a deputy mayor. He was always in the Jewish council, what the Jewish had, their own kehilla. So in everything, he was always the [NON-ENGLISH] in the, in the synagogue. Everything what had to be done, he was always very much involved.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you just mentioned the synagogue. Was there a synagogue or a stiebl in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a synagogue, and then we had two-- we called it [NON-ENGLISH]. It means houses of worship. We had the large house of worship and the smaller house of worship where the more religious Jews prayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did your father pray?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and then we also had three stieblech. And the stieblech was-- one was stiebl where all the handicraft people-- how you call the-- artisans work-- prayed. And there was a stiebl by the rabbi. And there was a stiebl by the dean, which he was assistant rabbi. My father was always in the large house of worship, where, so long as I remember, since I was born, he was the head of this this house of worship. It means he was the gabbai.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had a rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I remember his name. He was a very prominent rabbi from a very prominent family. His name was Yehil Teitelbaum. And the family Teitelbaum became very prominent because, now, we have a dynasty from Hungary. And we call them the Sutener dynasty, the very ultra-ultra-orthodox. And the originator of this dynasty was a son of our old rabbi from Kolbuszowa. And till today, their name is also Teitelbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Isn't that interesting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, tell me about your family life. Would you describe your family as very orthodox, traditional Jewish? How would you describe them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say that our family was very orthodox. My father was a follower of a very known rabbi. He was called the [? Czeguver ?] rabbi. And he used to go to him twice a year for the holidays. And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean when you say go to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Hasidim, the followers of the rabbi, usually went to the rabbi for a holiday, to celebrate with him, to pray with him. So my father used to go twice a year to him. And, also, this rabbi used-- came over to our town once in two years. And when he came to our town, all his followers from all the surrounding towns came to our town. And this was like a a major convention. You could see hundreds of hundreds Hasidic Jews in our town. And the rabbi went to the temple. And one day, he came back to where he was staying. And they danced on the street, and they sang in the street for hours. And, this, he did every two years. And, and, and my father was so involved with the rabbi, when I was 15 years, he even sent me to a school in the town where the rabbi lived and I was there-- in his yeshiva. It means a, a school for higher learning, Jewish learning. I was there about the year.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, Let's talk about your schooling because that's something that I'd like to discuss. Now, when you say that the family was orthodox, did your mother wear a sheitel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother wore a sheitel. And she was very, very religious because she came from a very, very Orthodox home too. In the home, the first three sisters wore sheitels. The last one, after a short while, she grew-- she had hair-- her own hair, and she didn't want to wear anymore a sheitel. But we were very-- as a matter of fact, my sisters were more religious than my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because-- because their husbands, my father always when-- by us, it was a, a matched--

speaker: interviewer
text: Marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marriage. And when he-- because he was well off, so he could give a good dowry, a high dowry. And the better the dowry was, a more important husband he could choose for the daughter. And because he gave, in this time, he always gave my sister $2,000 a dowry, was, was exceptional high. And my father, for this reason, he always picked out a husband for my sister who were grandchildren from very, very famous rabbis. So one, the older sister got a grandchild from the Samsa rabbi, which were famous. The second sister was from the Dinova rabbi. So each sister had somebody very important in Jewish life. And for this reasons, reason, the sisters were very, very orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: It makes a lot of sense.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, can you explain to us, please-- just give us a little bit of your own personal background in school. What age did you start?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, when I was three years, my father and mother took me, and my father covered me with a tallit, with a prayer shawl. And by three years, they took me to a Chaider. And this was the first time they cut my hair. Before children, boys, didn't have their hair cut. And then when they brought me to Chaider, naturally, children of my age were, were there. And everybody was crying. The first time, three year old, you bring them to a strange place. But what they did, my mother brought along with her candy, and raisins, and almonds. And as they set me down by the table in this old malamet, the old rabbi started to introduce me to the Jewish alphabet. So they, they== besides throwing raisins, and almonds, and candy on the prayer book, they also smeared my fingers with honey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sounds like an [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yes. And with honey. And they told me to lick the fingers. This was to show how the letters, the [NON-ENGLISH] the prayer book is sweet. And this was the first day of my Chaider, which later, I was sitting, crying, till they took me home. I was in this Chaider for about a year, or two years. And after each year, I advanced to a new teacher, to a new malamet, who had bigger, higher grades. Now, higher grades was when I was four, when I was five. We started to learn Humash, the Bible. And I went to this schools, this was a one-room school, practically no windows. But we were sitting and we learned. And then till six. When I was six, I started to attend public school. Public school, we had to go. And there-- this was a Poles and non-Jews-- I mean Poles and Jews, we were together in the same class. In the beginning, it was very hard because the Polish people didn't like Jewish, young Jewish with [INAUDIBLE] And we used to wear yarmulkes. So it was a lot of resistance from the children. But little by little, we got used to it. So I went to this public school for seven years, from one grade to the others. And also, after I started the public school, I enter the Talmud Torah. Talmud Torah is a higher class, higher class, in our town that we started to study the Talmud, in the Talmud Torah. And in school, I went to school till I was I think 12, 13 years. And then, later, it was finished. And I was the only-- and I was a very good student in school. Even the teachers didn't like Jewish boys because we had [INAUDIBLE] and yarmulkes and we were dressed different. But I always had a very good report cards. As a matter of fact, my report cards were mostly excellent from top to bottom, all subjects, which was very unusual because the teachers didn't like Jewish boys with [INAUDIBLE]. But they didn't have a choice and they gave me good grades.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you able to continue in this-- in the public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In public school, I continued till I finished seventh grades.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I wanted to go to a gymnasium, which is high school, but it was impossible for me because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it impossible?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the gymnasium, we had to go Saturday. And, naturally, I wouldn't dare. Or my father wouldn't let me or my sisters to go in a school where we had to attend Saturday and we had to write Saturday. So we didn't go. And I was the only one, when I finished seventh school, I came home and I cried the whole day. All my school friends, they were so happy and they compose songs and there were special songs. They are so happy that their schools are finished. I came home and I cried because I saw that this is the end of my secular education. And I wanted to go higher, but I knew I couldn't do it. So I joined-- I enrolled in a vocational school. They had in our town, a vocational school, which I had to go in the evening.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the evening, the vocational school was for two years. And I picked out-- there was different subjects. I picked out subjects-- bookkeeping and business courses.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after two years, I finished the vocational school. So I asked the principal if he would allow me to go an other two years in the same classes, just to go to school. So I went again to the same classes there the next year, I think, was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- and then when I was 15 years, my father, he always dreamt that I would be a scholar like him. He always wanted the sons should be very religious, which he was not successful. And he sent me to his rabbi, to Tarnow. There was a larger town where his rabbi, whose follower, my father, was, this was the Czeguver rabbi. And he sent me to his school. And this rabbi had a yeshiva, a school for higher learning. And I went to Tarnow to this yeshiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we talked just a little bit about your mother. She certainly had enough children at home to stay at home. But did she work outside of the home at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Outside, it's not like here that you have to travel to go to your job. See, our house, we had our business in our house. My father bought a two-story house. Which in our town, there were only five two-story houses.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he had a two-story house. And we had the store, and we had basements, warehouses. And we had in the back some warehouses. And we lived in the same house. So my mother, when she got out and she was in the kitchen, and this was part, she could see through the window if somebody's needed in the store. But during the week, she didn't bother too much with the store because she prepared the food. She baked her own things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, like you said, she had enough children. And then when some children were married, so she had her grandchildren. And the grandchildren were brought up by us in our house the same as the children. There was no difference by us when the sister got married, for instance. And we had the market day, and we needed help in our stores, that everybody worked in the store. Even they were married, they came to our store and worked like they wouldn't be married. They handled the money. They handled the, the selling and the packing. It was like a family business, that even when they were married, there was no difference. Everything was together. Like, for instance, the older sister, she married this very scholarly man. And my father promised him that he will be-- we call it in Jewish, we call kest. It means the daughter is marrying somebody and we take him in for a year to eat, to live, and we will support him. This was kest.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came in for a year and we gave him an apartment in our house on the second floor. And the end was that he was in this place for 22 years. He never moved out. He never paid rent. He never paid for, for food. Because, in a store like this, he-- they took anything they wanted.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were very happy. It was set. My mother was happy with it, my father was happy with this arrangements. We had enough. So all the sisters, after they married, they still were connected with our business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, can you remember-- it was a small town where half the people were Jewish, a town of 4,000. It was 2,000 Jews, 2,000 Poles. Do you remember any incidents of anti-semitism when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were a lot of incidents. For instance, our town was distinguished to have a pogrom. Not every town was so lucky to have a pogrom, but our town had a pogrom. When the Poles gained their independence in 1918 in the Versailles Treaty-- so after 150 years, under occupation of Russia, Austria, and Germany, so they wanted to celebrate. After all, to have independence is not every day. So what is there a better way then to celebrate than to have a pogrom. Naturally, not a big pogrom, because it was a small town. To have a small pogrom.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the 6th of May, we had a pogrom in our town, that the peasants and all the people from the town came. And they robbed, they cleaned out everything, and they killed nine Jews. And they wounded 400 Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the whole town was destroyed. After three days, they didn't have any more to rob, to plunder, so they went away. But, nevertheless, nine Jews were, were killed. So this was the beginning of the celebration of having an independent Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the exact date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember the exact day. I cannot forget because everybody knew this was the 6th of May, 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: 6Th of May.

speaker: interviewee
text: 6Th of May, 1919.

speaker: interviewee
text: And special for me, it's a very significant date because I was born on the 6th of May, but 1920, a year later. And the reason we-- everybody remembers, because till the Second World War, the Poles composed a song. There is a very famous song, a church song that the Poles sing when in May. And this song is named [POLISH]. It means the happy song of the 3rd of May. Because the 3rd of May was the anniversary of the Polish constitution. And this was the, the more important-- most important holiday, national holiday, like we have here the 4th of July.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so, they-- so they had a song. And they changed the words. And I would like to tell you the words because-- Can you hold one minute, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll continue with this in just one minute.
